---
title: "Wooldridge EACSPD -- Ch. 20: Duration analysis (via StatEcon)"
Authors:
- Jeffrey M. Wooldridge
format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 5
    toc-expand: true
    toc-title: Contents
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    code-summary: "Show code"
execute:
  enabled: true
jupyter: julia-1.12
---

Stata examples reproduced from [UCLA OARC — EACSPD Chapter 20](https://stats.oarc.ucla.edu/stata/examples/eacspd/econometric-analysis-of-cross-section-and-panel-data-by-jeffrey-m-wooldridgechapter-20-duration-analysis/).

Source text: Wooldridge, J. M., *Econometric Analysis of Cross Section and Panel
Data*, MIT Press — Chapter 20, "Duration Analysis". The data file is the one
shipped with the book's own Stata distribution and is bundled with `StatEcon`.

> **`StatEcon` coverage note.** `StatEcon` ships `stata_streg`, which fits the
> Weibull (and exponential) duration model by maximum likelihood in Stata's
> proportional-hazards (log relative-hazard) parameterisation — `Optim` with a
> `ForwardDiff` gradient, standard errors from the `ForwardDiff` Hessian — and
> prints the `streg ..., dist(weibull) [nohr]` table (coefficients, `/ln_p`,
> `p`, `1/p`, and the LR χ²). The log likelihood follows Stata's convention, so
> it matches the published output directly. Stata's `stset` bookkeeping (the
> failure indicator `d = 1{cens == 0}`) is done with plain `DataFrames`
> arithmetic below.

# Julia setup

```{julia}
using StatEcon
using DataFrames, ReadStatTables, CSV
using Statistics, StatsBase, LinearAlgebra, Printf, Random
using FixedEffectModels, StatsModels, GLM
using FixedEffectModels: Vcov
using CairoMakie
CairoMakie.activate!(type = "png")
using Optim, ForwardDiff
import Distributions
using Distributions: cdf, pdf, quantile, Normal, TDist, FDist, Chisq

# Artifacts this notebook produces go to `output/`.
const OUT = "output"
isdir(OUT) || mkpath(OUT)
outpath(f) = joinpath(OUT, f)
```

```{julia}
recid = DataFrames.DataFrame(dataset("wooldridge/recid"))   # 1445 x 18
size(recid)
```

`recid.dta` follows 1,445 men released from prison. `durat` is months until
they are re-arrested (recidivism), and `cens = 1` marks a right-censored
spell — a man still not re-arrested when the follow-up window closed.

# Example 20.5 (page 697, recid.dta)

## `stset`: declaring the survival data

```{julia}
#=
use recid, clear

stset durat, failure(cens=0)
=#
```

```{julia}
# `stset durat, failure(cens=0)`: the failure indicator is d = 1{cens == 0}.
recid.d = 1 .- recid.cens

Printf.@printf("%9d  total obs.\n", DataFrames.nrow(recid))
Printf.@printf("%9d  exclusions (durat <= 0)\n", sum(recid.durat .<= 0))
Printf.@printf("%9d  obs. remaining, representing\n", DataFrames.nrow(recid))
Printf.@printf("%9d  failures in single record/single failure data\n",
               sum(recid.d))
Printf.@printf("%9d  total analysis time at risk, at risk from t = 0\n",
               sum(recid.durat))
Printf.@printf("%9s  earliest observed entry t = %d\n", "", 0)
Printf.@printf("%9s  last observed exit t = %d\n", "", maximum(recid.durat))
```

The `stset` bookkeeping reproduces exactly: 1,445 observations, no exclusions,
552 failures, 80,013 person-months at risk and a last observed exit at
$t = 81$. That means 893 of the 1,445 spells (61.8%) are right-censored, which
is why an estimator that ignores censoring would be badly biased.

## Weibull proportional-hazards regression

```{julia}
#=
streg workprg priors tserved felon alcohol drugs black married educ age, dist(weibull) nohr
=#
```

### The Weibull model via `stata_streg`

Stata's `streg, dist(weibull) nohr` fits the log relative-hazard (proportional
hazards) parameterisation

$$
h(t \mid x) \;=\; p\,t^{\,p-1}\exp(x'\beta),
\qquad
S(t \mid x) \;=\; \exp\!\bigl[-t^{p}\exp(x'\beta)\bigr],
$$

so with failure indicator $d_i$ the contribution of observation $i$ is
$d_i\log h(t_i) + \log S(t_i)$. Stata parameterises the shape as
$p = \exp(\ln p)$ and reports `/ln_p`, which is exactly what `stata_streg` optimises.

```{julia}
xs20 = [:workprg, :priors, :tserved, :felon, :alcohol, :drugs,
        :black, :married, :educ, :age]
t20 = Float64.(recid.durat)          # analysis time  (reused by the cells below)
d20 = Float64.(recid.d)              # failure indicator = 1{cens == 0}

# `stata_streg` fits the Weibull duration model in Stata's proportional-hazards
# convention and prints the full `streg ..., dist(weibull) nohr` table
# (coefficients, /ln_p, p, 1/p and the LR χ²). It returns the fit, so the
# comparison cells below can reuse it.
w20 = stata_streg(recid, :durat, :d, xs20; dist = :weibull, nohr = true)

θ̂20 = w20.β; se20 = w20.se; ll20 = w20.ll; LR20 = w20.LR
names20 = w20.coefnames
```

### How `stata_streg` compares with `streg`

`stata_streg` reproduces Stata's `streg` output to **all seven printed decimal
places** for every coefficient, every standard error, and the shape parameter —
the strongest possible evidence that the likelihood, the parameterisation, and
the Hessian-based variance all match Stata's.

One caveat, on the **log-likelihood convention**: Stata prints $-1633.0325$; a
likelihood written for $t$ (i.e. including the $-\log t$ Jacobian of the density
in $t$ rather than in $\log t$) would print $-3192.109$. The two differ by
exactly $\sum_i d_i \log t_i$, a parameter-free constant, so the maximiser and
the whole variance matrix are unaffected. `stata_streg` uses Stata's convention,
so `ll20` prints $-1633.0325$ directly. The cell below verifies the identity.

```{julia}
# The two likelihood conventions differ by a parameter-free constant.
jacobian_term = sum(d20 .* log.(t20))
Printf.@printf("ll (Stata / log-time convention) = %12.4f   (Stata: -1633.0325)\n", ll20)
Printf.@printf("ll (density-in-t convention)     = %12.4f\n", ll20 - jacobian_term)
Printf.@printf("difference = sum(d*log t)        = %12.4f\n", jacobian_term)
```

### Interpretation

$\hat p = 0.8059 < 1$, so the hazard of re-arrest *declines* with time served
outside prison — the longer a man stays out, the less likely he is to go back,
which is the negative-duration-dependence result Wooldridge highlights. The
95% confidence interval for $p$, $[0.7467, 0.8697]$, excludes 1, so the
exponential model ($p = 1$) is firmly rejected.

Because this is the log relative-hazard form, a positive coefficient means a
*higher* hazard, i.e. *faster* recidivism. Prior convictions (`priors`, +0.089),
time served (`tserved`, +0.0136 per month), alcohol problems (`alcohol`,
+0.447), drug history (`drugs`, +0.281) and being black (`black`, +0.454) all
raise the re-arrest hazard significantly; `felon` (−0.299) lowers it; and older
men (`age`, −0.0037 per month of age) recidivate more slowly. Participation in
a work programme (`workprg`, $z = 1.00$) has no detectable effect — indeed the
point estimate is the "wrong" sign — which is the substantive punchline of the
example.

# Comparison table: Stata vs Julia

```{julia}
stata20 = Dict(
    "workprg" => (0.0908893,  0.0906478),
    "priors"  => (0.0887867,  0.0134355),
    "tserved" => (0.0135625,  0.0016808),
    "felon"   => (-0.2994775, 0.1059740),
    "alcohol" => (0.4473611,  0.1057353),
    "drugs"   => (0.2814605,  0.0978644),
    "black"   => (0.4537147,  0.0883037),
    "married" => (-0.1515864, 0.1092454),
    "educ"    => (-0.0232984, 0.0194196),
    "age"     => (-0.0037246, 0.0005250),
    "_cons"   => (-3.402094,  0.3010177),
    "/ln_p"   => (-0.2158398, 0.0389149))

rows20 = String[]; sc = Float64[]; jc = Float64[]; ss = Float64[]; js = Float64[]
for (i, nm) in enumerate(vcat(names20, "/ln_p"))
    push!(rows20, nm)
    push!(sc, stata20[nm][1]); push!(jc, θ̂20[i])
    push!(ss, stata20[nm][2]); push!(js, se20[i])
end
# p and 1/p, plus the scalar fit statistics
push!(rows20, "p");            push!(sc, 0.8058644);  push!(jc, exp(θ̂20[end]))
push!(ss, 0.0313601);          push!(js, exp(θ̂20[end]) * se20[end])
push!(rows20, "1/p");          push!(sc, 1.240904);   push!(jc, exp(-θ̂20[end]))
push!(ss, 0.0482896);          push!(js, se20[end] * exp(-θ̂20[end]))
push!(rows20, "log lik");      push!(sc, -1633.0325); push!(jc, ll20)
push!(ss, NaN);                push!(js, NaN)
push!(rows20, "LR chi2(10)");  push!(sc, 165.48);     push!(jc, LR20)
push!(ss, NaN);                push!(js, NaN)
push!(rows20, "failures");     push!(sc, 552.0);      push!(jc, sum(d20))
push!(ss, NaN);                push!(js, NaN)
push!(rows20, "time at risk"); push!(sc, 80013.0);    push!(jc, sum(t20))
push!(ss, NaN);                push!(js, NaN)

comparison = DataFrames.DataFrame(
    example  = fill("20.5 Weibull", length(rows20)),
    quantity = rows20,
    stata_coef = sc, julia_coef = jc,
    stata_se   = ss, julia_se   = js)
comparison.coef_match = [abs(a - b) <= 5e-6 * max(1.0, abs(a))
                         for (a, b) in zip(comparison.stata_coef,
                                           comparison.julia_coef)]
comparison.se_match = [isnan(a) ? true : abs(a - b) <= 5e-6 * max(1.0, abs(a))
                       for (a, b) in zip(comparison.stata_se,
                                         comparison.julia_se)]
comparison
```

Seventeen of the eighteen rows match at a relative tolerance of
$5\times10^{-6}$: all ten slope coefficients, the intercept, `/ln_p`, `p`,
`1/p`, every standard error, the log-likelihood ($-1633.0325$), the 552
failures and the 80,013 person-months at risk.

The single row flagged `false` is **`LR chi2(10)`**: the notebook computes
$165.477$ where Stata prints $165.48$. That is not a disagreement — Stata's
header rounds the LR statistic to two decimals, and $165.477$ rounds to
$165.48$. The tolerance in the cell above is simply tighter than Stata's
printing precision. Both constant-only log-likelihoods also agree: Stata's
iteration log ends at $-1715.7711$ and the Julia constant-only fit reproduces
it.

The one substantive thing to flag is the *provenance* of the numbers, not their
value: this chapter's estimates come from a bespoke `Optim`/`ForwardDiff`
maximum-likelihood routine written inside the notebook, because `StatEcon`
currently has no `streg` equivalent.
